entries.objective: Make sure I really understood the TBS deadline rule and whether it is fair to call TBS 'optimal'
entries.prompt: Explain the Total Bandwidth Server rule d_k = max(r_k, d_{k-1}) + C_k/U_s term by term. Is TBS optimal? Optimal for response time too?
entries.ai_output_summary: Walked through the rule and pointed out TBS is bandwidth-optimal (it reaches the U_p + U_s <= 1 bound) but NOT optimal for aperiodic response time -- servers like EDL/TB* do better there.

actions: Checked the response-time point against Spuri & Buttazzo (1994), which proposes the better servers.

issues: The first explanation just said 'optimal' with no qualifier, which is misleading

entries.reflection: I had written 'optimal in the EDF sense' in my draft. After checking the papers I changed it to 'bandwidth-optimal' and added a line that it is not response-time optimal. Good reminder to be precise instead of just copying a buzzword.

entries.objective: Find the original papers for the other servers I mention (Polling, Deferrable, Sporadic, CBS, timed automata) and write the short relevance notes the course asks for.
entries.prompt: Help me get the correct original references (authors, venue, year, DOI) for Deferrable Server, Sporadic Server, Constant Bandwidth Server and the UPPAAL / timed-automata background.
entries.ai_output_summary: Returned candidate references with details and a one-line note for each on how it relates to TBS.

actions: Compared each relevance note against the paper's own abstract.

issues: A few page numbers / venues were slightly off and I corrected them.
I only kept references I could actually open and confirm myself.

entries.reflection: It saved me time hunting DOIs, but I still had to verify each one. The 'note per reference' bit lines up with what the seminar wants in the .bib file, so I kept that.

entries.objective: Find the correct original reference for the optimality of EDF.
entries.prompt: What is the original reference that proves EDF is optimal?
entries.ai_output_summary: Suggested Dertouzos (1974), 'Control robotics: the procedural control of physical processes', as the EDF-optimality source.

actions: Searched the library and the literature databases for Dertouzos (1974).

entries.reflection: Even if the reference is probably real, I'm not citing what I can't actually verify. I attributed the EDF-optimality statement to the Buttazzo textbook instead, which I do have. This is the moment I trusted my own checking over the AI.

entries.objective: Figure out why my periodic generators stop producing jobs and how to make the model keep running while staying verifiable.
entries.prompt: My UPPAAL TBS model stops at HORIZON and the scheduler just loops on Done. How can periodic and aperiodic tasks keep being generated forever, and still be model-checkable?
entries.ai_output_summary: Explained that the Done/HORIZON branch freezes time, so generation stops. Suggested a relative-time encoding (bounded countdowns instead of a growing clock) to keep the state space finite, plus a sporadic nondeterministic aperiodic generator and the rate limit MIT >= C_k/U_s.
entries.reflection: This is where I learned the most. I finally understood the state-space explosion problem and why relative time keeps it finite. The continuous model makes my 'no deadline missed' result much stronger than the bounded run.

entries.objective: Check that my paper actually covers the parts the seminar guide asks for and that the argument flows.
entries.prompt: Here is my draft structure for the TBS seminar paper. Does it cover the maths basics, the formal algorithm, the runtime overhead, a UPPAAL implementation and an application example? Any weak spots?
entries.ai_output_summary: Confirmed the required parts were present, flagged a missing related-work paragraph and a hardcoded section cross-reference, and suggested tightening the limitations section.

actions: Decided myself which suggestions to take and which to drop.

issues: Some suggestions would have blown the 5-page limit, so I skipped them.

entries.reflection: Handy as a checklist against the requirements. I added a short related-work paragraph and fixed the cross-reference, but kept everything inside the page limit.

entries.objective: Clean up grammar and clarity in the sections I had written, without changing my wording too much.
entries.prompt: Check this paragraph for grammar and clarity but keep my own wording.
entries.ai_output_summary: Suggested small grammar and phrasing fixes, no new technical content.

actions: Read each suggested change and only kept the ones that did not change my meaning.

issues: A few suggestions made it sound too formal / not like me, so I dropped those.

entries.reflection: Just language cleanup. The ideas and the sentences are mine; I only fixed wording.

entries.objective: Get a first working UPPAAL model of the TBS with periodic + aperiodic generators and an EDF scheduler.	Claude (AI assistant)
entries.prompt: Build a UPPAAL timed-automata model of the Total Bandwidth Server: a PeriodicGenerator that releases a job every period, an AperiodicGenerator that assigns the TBS deadline d_k = max(r_k, d_{k-1}) + C_k/U_s, and a Scheduler that runs EDF one time unit at a time and flags deadline misses. Use integer (discrete) time.
entries.ai_output_summary: Produced three templates — PeriodicGenerator, AperiodicGenerator, Scheduler — with a committed-location chain (MissCheck→Release→Select→Dispatch→Run/Idle), integer TBS arithmetic C_k·den/num, and global arrays for task state.

actions: Loaded it in UPPAAL and ran the simulator.Checked the assigned deadlines came out 6, 12, 22 for my example.Compared the timeline against my hand calculation.	partially-acceptedL

entries.reflection: It gave me a running starting point instead of a blank page, but I only believed it after the simulator matched my own numbers. I had to understand every template before using it.

entries.objective: Make the model reproduce the exact EDF schedule and the exact TBS deadlines, and add verification queries.
entries.prompt: Make the TBS deadline integer-exact (C_k·den/num), break EDF deadline ties in favour of the server (aperiodic), detect deadline misses at every instant, and give me TCTL queries for deadlock-freedom, no missed deadline, and that the deadlines equal 6, 12, 22.
entries.ai_output_summary: Switched the deadline to integer arithmetic, set EDF selection so aperiodic wins ties (<=), added a per-instant miss check, and wrote 7 TCTL queries (deadlock, no-miss, reaches horizon, three deadline equalities, last job completes).

actions: Re-ran all 7 queries in UPPAAL — all passed.Manually checked the [0,24) timeline matches my hand-drawn schedule.Confirmed deadlines 6/12/22 via the equality queries.

issues: Duplicate figure labels and some naming I had to clean up myself.Had to confirm the tie-break choice was really what I intended.

entries.reflection: I learned how committed locations plus an integer clock reproduce the exact preemptive schedule. After the queries lined up with my own calculation I actually trusted the model.

entries.objective: My periodic generators stopped after the horizon and the scheduler just looped on Done; I wanted it to run forever and still be verifiable.
entries.prompt: My UPPAAL TBS model freezes at HORIZON — the scheduler loops on Done and generation stops. How can periodic and aperiodic tasks keep being generated forever and the model still be checkable?
entries.ai_output_summary: My UPPAAL TBS model freezes at HORIZON — the scheduler loops on Done and generation stops. How can periodic and aperiodic tasks keep being generated forever and the model still be checkable?

actions: Built the relative-time model and ran the 5 new queries in UPPAAL.Hand-traced the first ticks (τ0 at 0,4,8…; τ1 at 0,6,12…).Chose concrete integer bounds so UPPAAL accepted the variable ranges.

issues: I had to pick the variable bounds myself.Had to reason out why the rate limit is necessary (otherwise backlog → infinite deadlines).The absolute-deadline equality queries no longer apply, so I replaced them.

entries.reflection: This is where I learned the most — the state-space explosion problem and why relative time keeps it finite. The continuous model lifts my "no missed deadline" from one window to an unbounded, all-patterns guarantee.
